data.title: Enphase Energy: Sell On Weak Near-Term Outlook (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have discussed Enphase Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:ENPH) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier coverage of the company.

After the close of Tuesday's session, leading microinverter and battery storage solutions supplier Enphase Energy Inc. or "Enphase" reported another set of disappointing quarterly results with revenues and profitability coming in below consensus expectations:

However, reported results came in within the guidance ranges provided on the Q2/2024 conference call:

While revenues in the United States increased by approximately 43% on a sequential basis, sales in Europe were down by 15% relative to management's previously stated expectations of "a little down to flat".

With Enphase having entered additional European markets during the quarter, the magnitude of the sequential decline is eye-catching.

Even worse, sell-through of the company's products in Europe was down by 34% on a sequential basis, as outlined by management in more detail on the conference call:

The overall sell-through of our products in Europe was a little more down, 34% down in Q3 compared to Q2. While every country in Europe has its nuances, the overall business environment in the region is challenging. Power prices have declined from the early 2023-highs. The economic growth is slow and the consumer confidence is limited.

In addition, there continues to be regulatory uncertainty in the Netherlands, which has been one of Europe's largest markets in the past.

In France, expected utility rate cuts in early 2025 are likely to impact near-term demand.

Similar to last quarter, total sales benefited from a large increase in IQ Battery shipments:

During the quarter, battery sales got a one-time boost from increased channel shipments. However, with channel inventories now at healthy levels, this benefit won't repeat.

While micro-inverter sales were also up by more than 20% from the second quarter, the company is still operating at a tiny fraction of its global micro-inverter capacity, which currently stands around 7.25 million units per quarter.

During the quarter, Enphase generated $161.6 million in free cash flow and allocated $49.7 million to share buybacks:

Since inception of the company's up to $1 billion share repurchase program last year, Enphase has bought back approximately 3.69 million shares at an average price of $108.86. Remaining capacity under the program amounts to almost $600 million.

Enphase ended the second quarter with $1.77 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, up from the $1.65 billion reported at the end of Q2.

Debt remained virtually unchanged at $1.31 billion:

Please note that the company might be required to redeem $102.2 million in convertible notes in cash on March 1, 2025. While this minor debt maturity won't be a problem, the company will have to address an additional $632.5 million in convertible notes twelve months later.

However, with solid cash generation likely to persist, redemption of the 2026 convertible notes should not be an issue either.

In the earning release, Enphase also provided its outlook for the fourth quarter. Disappointingly, management does not expect further sequential improvement in sales:

While the U.S. business is projected to show incremental improvement, demand in Europe is expected to slow down even further. In addition, IQ Battery shipments are expected to decrease slightly on a sequential basis.

Please note that the company's Q4 revenue outlook is well below consensus expectations:

Even worse, given persistent issues in Europe, current consensus expectations of 40%+ year-over-year revenue growth in 2025 will likely have to come down materially.

On the conference call, management was optimistic about prospects for the U.S. business next year but this is not likely to fully offset persistent weakness in Europe.

During the questions-and-answers session, management basically admitted to previously stated normalized quarterly revenue assumptions being too high, at least in the near-term:

Philip Shen

Yeah. Thanks for taking my questions. First one is on -- in the past, you've talked about getting to a $450 million to $500 million sell-through demand run rate. And back in May, you thought it might be sometime in Q4. What's your thinking now in terms of when you can hit that on a quarterly basis? Is it sometime through '25, maybe Q2? Or could it be more in the back half? Thanks.

Badri Kothandaraman

Yes. I mean, look we talked about that, and we have had a few things there. We have had a few installer bankruptcies. I mentioned a big one. And we've had some headwinds. While the US is proceeding in the right direction, Europe is an entirely different story. So basically, a few months back, we didn't anticipate the installer bankruptcies, one in the US, we didn't anticipate the European business declining further. And that's reality right now.

Consequently, I would expect analysts to adjust their models and reduce price targets across the board over the next couple of weeks.

With business conditions in Europe unlikely to improve meaningfully anytime soon, I would expect the company's shares to remain in the penalty box for at least the next couple of quarters.

Given this issue, I am downgrading Enphase Energy's shares from "Hold" to "Sell".

Bottom Line

Enphase Energy reported disappointing Q3/2024 results and issued Q4 guidance well below expectations as business conditions in Europe continued to deteriorate during the quarter.

While the company's core domestic market is expected to recover further going into 2025, persistent weakness in Europe is likely to impact Enphase Energy's growth trajectory for the time being.

As a result, I would expect analysts to reduce price targets across the board, with the stock likely to remain in the penalty box until Europe starts to show signs of improvement.

For my part, I would not be surprised to see the company's shares moving to new 52-week lows sooner rather than later.

Consequently, I am downgrading Enphase Energy's shares from "Hold" to "Sell".

data.title: TransMedics' Organ Care: Critical Innovation With Lofty Expectations
data.description:
TransMedics Keeps the Beat Going with Organ Care and Soaring Revenues

TransMedics Group (NASDAQ:TMDX) specializes in the handling and transportation of vital organs like the heart, lungs, and liver. Its Organ Care System [OCS] provides extracorporeal warm perfusion [EWP] and real-time organ assessment. This enables longer preservation and improved viability of donor organs. TransMedic's OCS has achieved FDA approval in lung, heart, and liver transplantation after pivotal trials like INSPIRE, PROCEED II, and PROTECT. Peer-reviewed publications have supported OCS benefits over conventional cold storage and transportation of organs. However, its main benefit surrounds its ability to "expand the feasible geographic distance between (...) procurement and transplantation," thus expanding the donor poor. Estimates for the global transplantation market range between $10 billion and $15 billion. In the warm perfusion technology market, the company faces competition from OrganOx and XVIVO Perfusion in liver and lung transplantation, respectively. TransMedics is just beginning to tap into this market. Q2 revenue surged to $114.3 million, up from $52.5 million in Q2 2023. The net income of $12.2 million represented a net profit margin of 10.7%. After launching its own aviation services as a part of its National OCS Program (NOP) in 2023, TransMedics reports revenues under two categories: service (logistics, transportation, recurring, lower margin) and product (selling the OCS device and disposable components used in each transplant).

After summarizing the market potential, moat, and impressive financial performance in the early innings of warm perfusion technology, it's time to discuss some risks and limitations.

TransMedics’ Warm Perfusion: Hot Innovation or Lukewarm Market?

For one, OCS is "very expensive" and complex relative to alternatives like cold storage. Importantly, its cost-effectiveness remains uncertain. For example, in a June 2024 study of 13,930 transplant patients, portable ex vivo lung perfusion was found to be "not currently cost-effective at low-volume transplant centers in the United States." Moreover, normothermic regional perfusion [NRP] is emerging as a "cost-effective alternative in donation." This method involves restoring circulation to donor organs within the body by maintaining blood flow at physiological temperatures at death. This reduces the need for expensive external devices and consumables like those required by the OCS. Admittedly, the process is also complex and raises ethical questions. According to my understanding, the ethical concern is whether NRP "re-establishes" neurological function after "legal and medical death" via collateral flow (clamps are placed procedurally and strategically to prevent direct flow to the brain), potentially resulting in unnecessary suffering on the behalf of the donor. However, studies suggest no such incidences. It is estimated that around 11.4% of liver transplants come from donors using NRP, and there is a clear upward trend, although utilization is still relatively limited in the US. NRP can only be utilized in donation after circulatory death, which accounts for ~1/4 of all cases. Most donations are made after brain death.

According to a cost-utility analysis in the UK, OrganOx metra (for liver perfusion and transportation) is likely to be cost-effective and associated with better patient outcomes. Metra is approved in Europe and the U.S. and can preserve donated livers for up to 12 hours before transplantation, which is similar to OCS. OrganOx does not have a significant presence in the United States, so I do not expect it to "best" OCS in the EWP liver market.

The real question is how much demand will exist for EWP. My interpretation of the literature thus far suggests that EWP is useful as a "supplement," particularly in higher-risk cases with long geographical distances. In this niche, TransMedics has a considerable moat, especially as their services expand. Assuming EWP technology takes 25% of the total US transplant market (~20,000 annual transplants with livers making up the majority of organs) and an 80/20 market share between TransMedics and competition (e.g., OrganOx metra), this implies 4,000 annual TransMedics' transplants. This suggests that the company's TAM is approaching its halfway point.

Here are some key considerations.

  • TransMedics is not the only U.S. provider of EWP technology.
  • EWP is not definitively the preferred transplantation in all cases, but is best suited for high-volume centers where geographic reach is an important consideration.
  • NRP is more affordable, less complex, and possibly leads to better utility outcomes than alternative solutions, particularly for liver and heart transplants donated after circulatory death. Lending to its cost-effectiveness, it's also worth noting that NRP can preserve multiple organs at once due to the "simultaneous treatment of multiple organs."

TransMedics' Future Hinges on Sustained Growth and Profit Margins

I believe markets are generally efficient, making it crucial to understand the assumptions driving a stock's valuation. By identifying these assumptions, we can assess whether they are realistic and evaluate how key factors, such as changes in margins, may affect future returns.

Regarding margins, following the aviation acquisition, TMDX stock slumped considerably. Looking back, investors may have been concerned about the company's ability to turn profits from these operations. Although "services" growth has resulted in smaller profit margins, it is impressively already a profitable venture, buoying the stock.

TMDX trades at a P/E multiple [FWD] around 3x that of its peers but is also growing (2025) revenue 900% faster than its peers. TransMedics will not continue to grow as fast as it is now. As such, its P/E ratio will likely come down to earth. For TMDX to be a worthwhile investment at this price and given its risk, I believe that 2026-2029 revenue will need to grow at least 20%. The transplantation market by itself is growing at a CAGR of 8.5%, so this requires considerable growth in market share as well. 20% revenue growth would still support a high P/E multiple (i.e., 40) relative to the sector median of ~30.

To summarize, if TMDX can consistently achieve 20% annual revenue growth and 15% net profit margins, its expected returns warrant cautious investment ("hold"). TMDX becomes a "buy" if it becomes apparent they can achieve 25% revenue growth (estimated annual return bumps up to 14.3%) and/or better profit margins than 15% (e.g., a 20% net profit margin bumps estimated annual return to 17.2%). If TMDX cannot grow revenue at 20% and/or its net profit margins are lower than 15%, its stock is likely a "sell" at these prices. Coincidentally, Baird believes TMDX can achieve 25%+ revenue growth that supports an "outperform rating" with a price target for the stock at "$200."

Financial Health

As of June 30, TransMedics reported $362.751 million in cash. Total current assets were $509.265 million, while total current liabilities were just $54.329 million. This indicates a current ratio well over 2, which implies the company can readily cover any short-term obligations. TransMedics does owe $448.534 million in convertible senior notes and $59.217 million in "long-term debt."

In the first six months of the year, TransMedics generated $22.283 million from operations. Given the company's solid balance sheet and ability to generate profits, they shouldn't have any problems paying off their debt.

TMDX Stock: Risk/Reward Analysis and Investment Recommendation

TransMedics is doing an excellent job gaining market share in the $10 billion-plus transplant market. Their technology is important, and they are quickly establishing a niche in this market. Its financial and stock performance thus far have clearly indicated exceptional execution. However, investors should be aware of some potential risks. The most obvious is that TMDX is priced near perfection. The assumptions are becoming increasingly lofty, and it will be challenging, but not impossible, for management to meet them. Although EWP has advantages over cold storage in transplants, it is not yet widely used, and its cost-effectiveness is questionable. Furthermore, TransMedics faces competition in the EWP market, and NRP is an alternative solution that may be less expensive, simpler, and lead to better organ outcomes for up to 1/3 of all transplant cases (its utilization is expected to grow beyond 25%).

TMDX is a textbook Quadrant 1 investment (high risk, high reward) that could fit nicely into a barbell portfolio. Its high valuation, however, should be approached with caution ("hold"). I believe TMDX is fairly valued by the market, assuming it continues to experience robust revenue and net profit margin growth.
